coredata.dc:title: Advances in Red/Near infrared carbon dots in bioimaging, sensing, and phototherapeutic applications

Hatimuria, Madushmita

coredata.dc:description: Photoactive carbon dots (CDs) with emission in the spectral range of 600 nm to the near-infrared (NIR) region, referred to here as R/NIR CDs, are zero-dimensional nanomaterials that demonstrate good solubility in water, better photostability, and the ability to manipulate their surface. They offer superior biocompatibility for use in next-generation sensing, drug delivery, and treatment systems. Moreover, due to minimal photoluminescence (PL) interference from R/NIR CDs, along with deep tissue penetration ability and resistance to photon-induced damage, they have gained substantial popularity in challenging sectors such as tumor growth monitoring. This review captures the recent progress in this area, discussing important reports on bio and chemical sensing using R/NIR CDs, and intriguing in vivo bioimaging studies targeting tumor and cancer cells. Subsequently, the current progress in the field of phototherapy using these CDs is illustrated. We hope that this review will contribute to current advancements and assist researchers in inventing new CDs with improved properties for clinical applications.

head.ce:title: Advances in Red/Near infrared carbon dots in bioimaging, sensing, and phototherapeutic applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoactive carbon dots (CDs) with emission in the spectral range of 600 nm to the near-infrared (NIR) region, referred to here as R/NIR CDs, are zero-dimensional nanomaterials that demonstrate good solubility in water, better photostability, and the ability to manipulate their surface. They offer superior biocompatibility for use in next-generation sensing, drug delivery, and treatment systems. Moreover, due to minimal photoluminescence (PL) interference from R/NIR CDs, along with deep tissue penetration ability and resistance to photon-induced damage, they have gained substantial popularity in challenging sectors such as tumor growth monitoring. This review captures the recent progress in this area, discussing important reports on bio and chemical sensing using R/NIR CDs, and intriguing in vivo bioimaging studies targeting tumor and cancer cells. Subsequently, the current progress in the field of phototherapy using these CDs is illustrated. We hope that this review will contribute to current advancements and assist researchers in inventing new CDs with improved properties for clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fascinated by the unique combination of zero-dimensional size, novel luminescent properties, biocompatibility and greater stability, significant interest is paid towards carbon dots (CDs) in the recent decade [1,2]. CDs are prepared from both chemical and biological sources, many of which contains N, S atoms besides carbon and usually they are synthesized by using either “top-down” or “bottom-up” approaches [3,4]. These materials are usually less than 10 nm in diameter with tailored electrical and optical properties [2]. As it contains a sp2 hybridised carbon at the centre, and functional groups (–COOH, –NH2, and –OH, etc.) rich shell, it offers tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, high photostability, water dispersibility, and low toxicity making the CDs promising nanomaterials in the area of bio-imaging, sensing, catalysis, and optoelectronics [2]. However, the majority of the reported CDs, have strong UV absorption as well as visible photoluminescence that appears at the lower region than 600 nm. As a result of this emissive property, only a handful reports are known which are also limited by the significant tissue absorption, high auto-fluorescence interference, low tissue penetration, and substantial photodamage to the cells and tissues. So, there was a strong demand to prepare CDs, which emits in red/near-infrared (R/NIR) spectral region upon long wavelength excitation, should be able to replace the traditional CDs in the area of biochemical and biomedical applications [3].

To better understand the components that affect the optical and structural features of R/NIR-CDs, researchers have also focused more on the synthesis and fluorescent mechanisms of these materials. The complex internal microstructures of CDs and the luminescent chemical origin that include the state of the carbon core, size, surface/molecular state, as well as cross-linked enhanced emission (CEE) effect which is the result of partial conversion of sp3 carbon to sp2 carbon and because of the heterogeneous impurities. Most of the studies that were published proved that the carbon shell (surface state) and core, both aid in the fluorescent property of CDs and its emission in the R/NIR region was predominantly influenced by their conjugated structure and oxidation states of the surface. So, longer wavelength emission is possible due to the surface oxidation and the presence of sizeable number of conjugated molecular structures (especially those that contained graphitized nitrogen) [5].

R/NIR CDs have significant benefits over similar other (green and yellow) CDs, including deep tissue penetration, reduced autofluorescence interference, and less photodamage to biological samples. Extensive research efforts have been committed to advancing these CDs applications. Consequently, new development on the R/NIR-CDs applications in the disciplines of biosensing, bioimaging, and therapy [6,7] has been checked.

R/NIR-CDs are used as sensor in vitro and in vivo as it possesses significant advances, such as, tunable surface modification, small size, great chemical stability, etc. In recent years there are several literatures which reported that R/NIR based CDs could be applied for the identification and quantification of metal ions, intracellular pH and small biomolecules [6].

One of the most significant application of CDs is in the area of bioimaging as they are smaller in size, have great photochemical stability, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and substantial MRI relaxivity of CDs which are doped with some heteroatom. As majority of the CDs, which are reported in literature mostly response only in UV and visible region, it limits their applications in bioimaging. With a low cytotoxicity, R/NIR-CDs are remarkable exception to this trend as it can penetrate deep into the cells with minimum photodamage [6,8].

These distinctive characteristics of R/NIR-CDs offer a significant promise in the treatment and diagnosis of cancer. As these have excellent photothermal conversion ability and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation capacity, these can be used as agents in photodynamic therapy (PDT) or photothermal therapy (PTT). Their possible toxicity and biodegradation mechanism, however, are not clear yet. The metabolic mechanisms via which CDs are eliminated from the body have been studied minimally. Still, some literature suggests that CDs can be digested and removed by several mechanisms, including phagocytosis, lymphatic drainage, hepatic metabolism, and renal excretion. The metabolic pathways for CDs release can change based on their route of administration and physicochemical nature [9].

Here, in this review, we are mainly focusing on the recent advancements of R/NIR-CDs in this rapidly evolving field of medical treatment and diagnosis. We mostly covered the recent advancements in biosensing, bioimaging, tumor theranostics and phototherapy employing R/NIR-CDs.

Recent popularity of the photoluminescent sensors have become a crucial tool to identify physiologically significant changes both in vivo and in vitro [9]. Deep red fluorescent carbon dots (R/NIR-CDs), which have more than 600 nm emission, comes under luminescent nanomaterials are versatile in nature. Such reports are included in this review. The R/NIR-CDs-based sensors which are based on analyte-induced luminescent quenching, enhancement, or displacement, have great selectivity for intracellular pH, metal ions, organophosphorus pesticides and small organic compounds [3].

Although, metallic ions are essential for many chemical and biological activities, their extensive use have negative effect on human and the environment [10]. Therefore, metal ion detection in aqueous medium using R/NIR-CDs as fluorescent nanosensor has been developed [11]. Also, selective binding capacity of R/NIR-CDs to the metal ions results distinctive change in the emission spectrum. By detecting this shift in fluorescence and comparing the findings with the known concentrations of metal ions, the assessment of the unknown metal ion concentration can be done [9].

As R-CDs offer less tissue photodamage and can penetrate deep into the organ. Hydrothermally synthesized CDs from citric acids emit red colour (excitation at 610 nm and emission at 632 nm) both in solution and solid states as reported by Gao et al. They observed that the intensity of fluorescence emission of the CDs remains nearly constant for other metal ions, excluding Au3+, Pt2+, and Pd2+. The noble metal ions effectively reduce the fluorescent intensity of the CDs. Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+ detection limit was calculated to be 0.886, 3.03, and 3.29 µM, respectively (Table 1). When they used these CDs into the PC12 cell for metal detection, it was observed that the fluorescent intensity decreases with the increase in Pt2+ concentration indicating the Pt+2 sensing ability of the CDs [13].

B, N & S doped R-CDs can detect Fe3+ by using fluorescent at 600 nm (when irradiated with excitation wavelength of 500 nm) and colorimetric dual mode was synthesized by Liu et al. using the hydrothermal route in the presence of 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride as well as 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid. With a detection limit of 90 nM (Table 1) and concentration range of 0.3–––546 µM, the synthesized R-CDs exhibit high sensitivity to Fe3+ for the fluorescence quenching in aqueous solution. The functional groups of R-CDs as well as Fe3+ forms a stable nonfluorescence complex that allows this sensitivity. The visual detection ability of Fe3+ using this nanosensor through the solution's visible colour changes (from red to blue) in the presence of sunlight (without separate ultraviolet excitation) is more appealing (Fig. 1 ). Additionally, the rapid response from the R-CDs enable the detection procedure can be finished within a minute. The selectivity experiments assessing the synthesized CD were conducted to ascertain its efficacy towards Fe3+ in the presence of potential interfering substances commonly found in urine and serum samples. These interfering substances encompassed various metal cations (Co2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Fe2+), amino acids (threonine, tryptophan, L-tyrosine, lysine, arginine, glutamate, glycine, L-cysteine, and alanine), and glutathione (GSH). Analysis revealed that the interfering substances exhibited negligible impact on the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the synthesized CDs, apart from Fe2+, which induced a slight quenching effect on fluorescence. To extend the assessment of their practical utility, the synthesized CDs nanosensors were employed for the detection of Fe3+ in human urine and serum samples. Prior to analysis, the urine and serum samples underwent a 100-fold dilution. Subsequently, the diluted samples were enriched with different concentrations of standard Fe3+ solution. The experiment demonstrated favourable recoveries and elevated analytical precision. These findings substantiate the reliability and feasibility of employing the as synthesized CDs-based nanosensors for the surveillance of Fe3 + ions in biological specimens [14].

Liu and colleagues developed a ratiometric fluorescent test paper by using blue-CDs (B-CDs) and R-CDs (The R-CDs fluorescence spectra showed a peak emission at 615 nm with an excitation of 360 nm.) mixture as ink and then jet printing on paper. This fluorescent test paper can be used for the semiquantitative assessment of Cu2+ as it displayed red-blue dose-sensitive colour change. The dose-discerning ability of the test paper for Cu2+ is 25 nM (Table 1), and it can be detected by bare eye. The colour change on the test paper is the result of quenching of B-CDs blue fluorescence in presence of Cu2+ and it takes 2 min to shift the colour from blue - red. When test was carried out on the water samples sourced from tap and lake, it was found that the visual effects in both the cases are similar, and it can detect Cu2+ ions in high accuracy (without any interferences) ( Fig. 2
) [15].

Zhang and co-workers prepared a sulphur and nitrogen doped red-emissive CDs (or N, S-CDs) by using solvothermal method where L-cystine, o-phenylenediamine, and ethanol was used as precursor. The synthesized N, S-CDs can detect Ag+. After quenching, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs decreased by 56.5 % in presence of Ag+. It demonstrates that as-synthesized N, S-CDs fluorescence has quick reflex to Ag+ with linear response in the range of 0 to 100 µM (Table 1) [16].

The N-doped CDs (NCDs) synthesized by Song and co-workers can be used as a probe for the detection of intracellular Fe3+ (excitation at 600 nm and emission at 646 nm). The presence of Fe3+ quench the photoluminescence intensity of the NCDs effectively. The Fe3+ ions may coordinate with the amino groups after its interaction with the hydroxyl groups which are phenolic. Plausibly, PL quenching occur due to the excited electrons successfully transferring to the half-filled 3d orbitals of the Fe3+ ion. When Fe3+ ions are present, the average lifespan of the fluorescence degradation is decreased from 6.7 ns to 2.8 ns at 520 nm, which concluded that both NCDs surface and Fe3+ underwent dynamic quenching. With rising Fe3+ concentrations, the PL intensity of NCDs at 623 nm steadily dropped proving that the intensity quenching is concentration dependent. The probe has a low detection limit of 0.52 μM (Table 1), and 1 μM − 250 μM linear detection range for Fe3+ [17].

At 360 nm excitation, the CDs-AuNCs (carbon dots-gold nanocluster) developed by Xie et al. exhibit dual emission at 440 and 655 nm. While it has two emission signals, only the signal at 655 nm is quenched by the presence of Hg2+ and it decrease linearly with the increase of Hg2+ concentration. In the range of 2–––15 nM, the PL intensity ratio and Hg2+ concentration exhibits a good linear relationship and has a detection limit of 0.73 nM (Table 1). As the two emission has a large gap, the colour change exhibited due to the smaller Hg2+ concentration can be detected by bare eyes. The CDs-AuNCs is highly selective for Hg2+ even when the test was done using the tape water [18].

Several biological activities, such as, cellular proliferation, endocytic processes, apoptosis, enzyme activity, and signal transduction are all closely correlated with intracellular pH. In the recent decade, the use of fluorescent probes has emerged as a crucial technique for measuring intracellular pH [19]. Bai et al. developed a solvent responded and solvent controlled strategies for the preparation of multi-colour CDs which can be applied as a fluorescent probe for intracellular pH detection. The synthesis was completed by varying the solvents throughout the reaction or after its completion. The synthesized R-CDs have low fluorescence intensity (emission at 609 nm) in the highly acidic zone (pH: 1–––2), but the intensity increases rapidly as the pH rises to 3, which remain nearly constant till pH 10 region (Table 2 ). Under the natural sunlight, when the pH was reduced from 3 till 1 (more acidic condition), the reddish-brown coloured R-CDs solution transform into lilac, indicating that R-CDs can be utilized as “naked-eye” pH sensor. When interrogated with UV-light, the R-CDs solutions exhibit two different colours, red in case of pH range 3–––10 and green in pH 1–2 range (Table 2) [1].

Ci et al. prepared a NIR-II fluorescent Fe-doped CDs (Fe-CDs) (excitation 830 nm and emission 1000 nm) by using o-phenylenediamine, dopamine hydrochloride (DA) and FeCl3·6H2O (dopant) in a facile one-pot hydrothermal treatment. Due to the gradually increased CDs aggregation, the fluorescent intensity decreases linearly with the increase in pH in the range of 2–––6 (Table 2). As a result, Fe-CDs have a great potential for the accurate in vivo pH monitoring as their fluorescence signal remained stable even in presence of interfering chemicals including, glutathione, vitamin C. This Fe-CDs have been utilised as a NIR-II fluorescent sensor can be used to detect in vivo gastric pH changes and its real time monitoring (in a mouse model) [20].

The red-emission N, S-CDs, prepared by Zhang et al. can be used as a possible fluorescent pH sensor for living cells and environmental samples as they demonstrate outstanding pH responsiveness and reversible fluorescence throughout a broad pH value range of 1–––13 (excitation 600 nm and emission at 648 nm) (Table 2) [16].

By using hydrothermal process, Ye et al. synthesize a red emissive two photon fluorescence CDs (at 573 nm excitation, it has an emission at 640 nm) which is pH sensitive (pH-CDs). The prepared pH-CDs possess various exceptional qualities, such as, great selectivity, remarkable photostability, and low cytotoxicity, are present in pH-CDs. The fluorescence intensity reduces linearly with the increase of pH value from 1 to 9 and offers a good linear relationship in the pH range of 3.5–––6.5 (Table 2) which can monitor the pH level in living cells. This pH-CDs can be applied for the detection and display of pH variation in live tissues, cells, and zebrafish [19].

The CDs based fluorescence sensor synthesized by Song et al. can also be used for the detection of pH 1.5–––5 with excitation at 380 nm and emission at 624 nm (Table 2). The red fluorescence emission and pH displays a negative correlation under UV light, and the solution exhibit a gradual colour change from blue to pink [21].

Organic molecules, such as, amino acids, can be detected by using R/NIR-CDs [22]. Song et al. prepared a fluorescence sensor that can detect lysine in “turn-on” mode at the pH range 1.5–––5 ratiometrically. The sensor could also be used for the detection of lysine at pH 7.4 as a single wavelength (624 nm). The solution containing fluorescent CDs changes colour gradually from pink to purple under UV-light with the addition of lysine. The detection limit of lysine by using this sensor is as low as 94 nM (Table 3 ) and it takes 2 min to complete the detection process [21].

For the detection of glucose, Zhang et al. prepared a self-ratio fluorescence probe from their synthesized R-CDs. The probe can detect glucose with high sensitivity. As the glucose concentration increases, 610 nm and 650 nm fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs decreases linearly. It has a detection limit of 937.95 nM (Table 3). Concentration of glucose can be directly visualized by using this sensor as the R-CDs dispersion colour changes from orange to red to blue to violet with the increase in glucose concentration. The test paper prepared by using the synthesized R-CDs changes colour drastically under UV light in response to glucose concentration change [5].

A dual-mode probe that uses both PA (photoacoustic) and fluorescence was developed by Xu et al. for the targeted detection of ascorbic acid (AA). To implement the design, red-emissive carbon dots (RCDs) were hybridized with cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH), which was used as a multifunctional platform (FL quencher, PA contrast agent, and selective oxidant to AA). In the presence of AA, CoOOH is reduced to Co2+ and R-CDs are released, which in turn causes the decrease of PA and increase in fluorescence intensity. The sensor has a detection limit of 150 nM (Table 3) for AA and it can also detect intracellular AA [23].

The development of R/NIR-CDs make it possible to detect different metal ions, with different types of R/NIR-CDs showing high sensitivity and selectivity for specific ions, such as, Au3+, Pt2+, Fe3+, Ag+, etc. It can also be used in the detection of intracellular pH and to monitor biological processes if used as a fluorescent probe. These developed probes exhibit low cytotoxicity, making it possible to use in living cells and tissues. Additionally, R/NIR-CDs work as an effective detector for organic molecules, such as, amino acids and glucose, providing sensitive and selective detection methods for various biochemical species.

Synthesis of the exact R/NIR-CDs for the targeted applications have not been properly known till date. For metal ion sensing, a robust yet stable R/NIR-CD with distinctive emission ability needs to be prepared for practical applications. Such type of emissive CDs has not been reported so far.

Bioimaging is a non-invasive technique for visualizing the inner biological activity of any species or organs using light. It can produce real-time, high-resolution images and detect tumor cells or other diseases [24]. Nowadays, bioimaging using near-infrared (NIR) light-emitting fluorescent probes is at the forefront due to its ability for deep tissue penetration, lower tissue damage, high spatial resolution, and minimal autofluorescence [25].

As the literature suggests, there are many fluorescent probes, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic fluorescent dyes (FDs), that were often used for bioimaging in the past. However, they have several limitations, such as high cytotoxicity, poor hydrophilicity, and low photostability [25,26]. Recently, carbon dots have gained interest as fluorescent probes in bioimaging due to their outstanding properties, such as low cost, low cytotoxicity, high photo-stability, highly flexible surface modification, good water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [27,28]. NIR-emitting carbon dots in the range of ≥ 700 nm for bioimaging is discussed below using different bioimaging techniques, including fluorescence (FL) imaging, photoacoustic (PA) imaging, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).

Furthermore, the investigation was expanded to include two different injection routes, as depicted in Fig. 4
and Fig. 5 , by Li et al. These routes - subcutaneous and tail intravenous injections were explored for real-time in vivo NIR fluorescence bioimaging in mice using PVP-carbon dots. The results indicated that, in the presence of an aqueous solution of PVP-carbon dots, tail intravenous injection yielded enhanced NIR fluorescence compared to subcutaneous injection. This enhancement was observed under Ex/Em-671/750 nm laser excitation [29].

Li et al. synthesized near-infrared (NIR) emitting carbon dots from watermelon juice, as indicated in Table 4. These carbon dots exhibited luminescence in the range of 900–––1200 nm, boasting high biocompatibility and a substantial quantum yield of 0.4 %. In their study, in vivo NIR bioimaging was conducted in mice using these NIR-emitting carbon dots, utilizing 808 nm laser excitation and a 1000–––1400 nm bandpass filter. Upon injection, a robust NIR signal promptly emerged from the mouse kidney within one minute, intensifying over the subsequent 30 min. However, after one hour, the signal began to diminish, eventually becoming extremely faint after six hours. This behaviour indicated the swift renal clearance of the carbon dots. Investigating the primary excretion route, the researchers employed NIR bioimaging to examine the bladder and liver of the mouse. A highly intense signal was observed from the bladder, while the liver exhibited a weaker signal initially. Interestingly, the liver signal gradually increased over time, peaking after 24 h post-injection, before starting to decline after 48 h and persisting for up to 7 days. This pattern suggested the primary excretion of carbon dots through a hepatobiliary route. Expanding the study to other organs, the excretion mechanism was probed in the liver, heart, spleen, lung, and kidney of the mouse using the same bioimaging system. A strong fluorescence signal was evident in the liver and kidney, while the spleen showed a brief but weak signal, disappearing after 12 h. Based on the NIR bioimaging results, the rapid decline in fluorescence intensity in the kidney indicated predominant renal excretion. Consequently, this research highlighted that carbon dots smaller than 6 nm have the potential to facilitate swift renal excretion in mice [28].

Sun et al. prepared red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) by using citric acid and formamide as sources. They conjugated the dots with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and investigated the bioimaging properties of the R-CDs in MCF-7 and HeLa cells. The cells were incubated with R-CDs for 4 h, and confocal fluorescence microscopy was used for bioimaging under Ex/Em = 543/550–––750 nm (Table 4). As a result, a bright red emission was observed from both cell types, confirming that R-CDs were able to penetrate the cells. Furthermore, they were observed to penetrate the nuclear pore complex of both cell lines. Two-photon excitation fluorescence (TPEF) bioimaging was also performed in living cells to investigate the drug delivery action of FITC-conjugated R-CDs under Ex/Em = 543/550–––750 nm in MCF-7 cells. During the observation, both red (R-CDs) and green (fluorescein) emissions were clearly observed from the cells. This indicates that R-CDs have the potential to effectively carry FITC in living cells and can act as drug delivery agents [32].

Pan et al. synthesized NIR-CDs160 (near-infrared carbon dots) from glutathione and formamide. These NIR-CDs160 were then conjugated with fluorescein isothiocyanate to prepare NIR-CDs160-FITC. The researchers examined these carbon dots for fluorescence bioimaging. They performed both two-photon fluorescence and one-photon fluorescence bioimaging using NIR-CDs160 in MCF-7 cells. The excitation sources were an 850 nm femtosecond laser pulse for two-photon fluorescence and 550–––750 nm for one-photon fluorescence (Table 4). In both cases, a deep red emission was observed from the MCF-7 cells, confirming that the prepared carbon dots have the ability for fluorescence bioimaging. Furthermore, NIR-CDs160 were investigated as drug delivery agents for FITC in living cells. This investigation used one-photon fluorescence bioimaging in MCF-7 cells under excitation of 550–––750 nm. The researchers observed a deep red emission from NIR-CDs160 and a bright green emission from fluorescein in the MCF-7 cells (Figure 7 ). As a result, the authors concluded that NIR-CDs160 can effectively carry FITC in living cells and serve as an efficient carrier for drug delivery [33].

Geng et al. engineered p-n junction NIR phosphorescence-emitting carbon dots (p-n-CDs) at 760 nm with the highest phosphorescent efficiency of 17.6 %. They designed a CD-based theranostic platform (p-n-CD@CCM) using cancer cell membrane (CCM). In vivo NIR bioimaging was conducted on mice with 143B and HeLa tumors using free p-n-CDs, p-n-CD@143B, and p-n-CD@HeLa. The imaging was performed under excitation at 700 nm (Table 4). For p-n-CDs, no significant aggregation was observed at the tumor sites in mice due to rapid renal excretion. However, for p-n-CD@143B and p-n-CD@HeLa, significant aggregation was observed at the 143B and HeLa tumor sites in mice, respectively, due to the homotypic recognition nature of CCM. The highest NIR fluorescence was observed in both tumor areas 12 and 24 h after intravenous injection of the carbon dots. Based on these observations, the authors concluded that p-n-CD@CCM demonstrates excellent in vivo bioimaging capabilities with tumor-specific recognition [34].

Jia et al. synthesized magnetofluorescent Mn-Carbon dots (Mn-CDs) using manganese (II) phthalocyanine (Mn-Pc) through a solvothermal method. They investigated the NIR fluorescence bioimaging properties of Mn-CDs in 4 T1-tumor-bearing nude mice, as well as in the major organs of the mice including the heart, liver, spleen, kidneys, and lung. This investigation was conducted under 690 nm laser excitation (Table 4). The results revealed a gradual increase in the NIR fluorescence signal, with the highest accumulation of Mn-CDs observed at the tumor sites after 6 h of intravenous injection. Additionally, when comparing the accumulation of Mn-CDs in the major organs of the mice, a greater accumulation was observed in the tumor site after 6 h of injection. Notably, among the major organs, the liver and lung emitted the highest NIR fluorescence signal, attributed to macrophage uptake in the reticuloendothelial system. Over time, the signals from the major organs gradually decreased and completely disappeared after 14 days post-injection. The study concluded that Mn-CDs demonstrate exceptional potential as agents for in vivo fluorescence bioimaging and can be employed for in vivo photodynamic therapy (PDT). Furthermore, the optimal timing for PDT was determined to be after 6 h of intravenous injection of Mn-CDs [35].

From the above discussion, it is concluded that the prepared carbon dots are NIR/R-active and have excellent properties towards fluorescence (FL) bioimaging along with drug delivery as well as cell apoptosis properties, which is very helpful in the clinical applications. Additionally, most reported CDs injection route into the model was intravenous, which was the most effective route for bioimaging other than other routes. However, gavage route also had shown good efficiency towards bioimaging but not much studies are reported regarding gavage route. However, most of the carbon dots prepared from the chemical sources other than green source. Therefore, more studies are required to explore the different routes of CD injection into the model as well as possibilities of using green sources to synthesize the NIR/R-CDs for clinical applications.

Lan et al. used hydrothermal method to engineer high fluorescence carbon dots from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and Na2SO3 with absorption range up to 1100 nm. After preparing, they investigated the in vivo photoacoustic (PA) imaging capability of prepared carbon dots in 4 T1 tumor-bearing nude mice at different time intervals (i.e., 0 to 8 h). Results had shown that after receiving the intravenous injection of carbon dots increases the PA signal intensity in tumor region with time and attain maximum value at approximately 6 h of post-injection, which indicates that prepared carbon dots are highly efficient for PA imaging [36].

Yang et al. prepared a manganese doped nigrosine originated carbon dots (Mn-NCDs) by a hydrothermal method with emission wavelength of 653 nm. After preparing the Mn-NCDs, in vivo photoacoustic (PA) imaging was conducted to detect the Mn-NCDs accumulation in 4 T1 breast cancer cells of mice at two-time intervals (i.e., 4 and 24 h). As a result, they observed that after 24 h of intravenous injection of Mn-NCDs a stronger photoacoustic signal from the tumor sites. From which the authors concluded that prepared carbon dots are promising agents for bioimaging [37].

Jia et al. synthesized Hypocrella bambusae carbon dots (HBCDs) by solvothermal method from Hypocrella bambusae (a parasitic fungus in bamboo) with broad absorption (350–––800 nm) and red-light emission (maximum peak at 610 nm). The authors used HeLa cells of mice to perform in vivo photoacoustic (PA) imaging by using prepared HBCDs at different time points (i.e., 0, 2, 4, 8, 12 and 24 h) and observed that the PA signal increases rapidly till 8 h of intravenous injection of HBCDs and attained maximum at 8 h after that starts to decrease gradually. Therefore, it is concluded that the prepared HBCDs has capability to perform bioimaging [38].

From the above studies, we can conclude that the prepared carbon dots are NIR/R-active and have excellent properties towards photoacoustic (PA) bioimaging in the tumor sites. In most of the studies, PA signal of NIR/R-CD reached maximum in less than 8 h. Therefore, more studies are needed to explore the highest possible time for PA signals.

In 2018, Jia et al. prepared magnetofluorescent Mn-Carbon dots (Mn-CDs) from manganese (II) phthalocyanine (Mn-Pc) using a solvothermal method. They investigated in vivo T1-weighted magnetic resonance (MR) imaging in 4 T1-tumor-bearing nude mice to assess the retention of the Mn-CDs assembly within the tumor site, comparing it with a free CDs assembly (control group). The authors observed that after 6 h of intravenous injection of Mn-CDs, the intensity of the MR signal increased by approximately 320 %. In contrast, there was no observable change in the MR signals of the control group after intravenous injection. Based on these findings, the authors concluded that Mn-CDs serve as excellent and efficient agents for MR imaging applications, as well as for facilitating in vivo photodynamic therapy (PDT) [35].

Du et al. synthesized gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-doped CDs) by using one-step hydrothermal approach. At first, they compared the contrast enhancement capability of prepared Gd-doped CDs with magnevist by using MR imaging in different major parts (kidney, bladder, and heart) of the mice body. T1-weighted images were collected after the intravenously injection of Gd-doped CDs into the mice at different time points. Results had shown that after 10 min of intravenously injection, contrast enhancement was observed and it maintained upto 6 h in the case of Gd-doped CDs. Whereas after 6 h, magnevist contrast enhancement was started to disappear. Which indicates that Gd-doped CDs possess longer retention time in vivo. Again, they performed MR imaging on subcutaneous and in situ liver cancer models to investigate the capability of prepared Gd-doped CDs to detect the hepatocellular carcinomas (HCCs). After intravenous injection of the Gd-doped CDs, in the subcutaneous tumor region they observed that the tumor region become brighter than the surrounding tissue and the reticuloendothelial system (RES) reached drastically to 1.93 and 1.74 with coronal and transverse scanning. In the case of in situ tumor region appeared washout and the reticuloendothelial system (RES) decreased to 0.74 and 0.77 with the coronal and transverse scanning respectively. Overall, these observations concluded that the prepared Gd-doped CDs highly efficient for MR imaging [39].

Based on the above studies, it is concluded that the prepared carbon dots are NIR/R-active and have outstanding properties towards magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). However, in these studies only intravenous route was used to inject the prepared carbon dots into the models. Therefore, additional studies are required to explore the different routes of injection of carbon dots and their effect on the MR-imaging.

Phototherapy is a form of treatment where a variety of medical disorders can be treated by the exposure of light using a controlled wavelength. Recently, photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), the two major phototherapeutic techniques, are attracting tremendous attention of researchers for the treatment of cancers as well as other infectious diseases owing to their non-invasive nature, low side effects, less cytotoxicity and no drug resistance [40]. Besides, phototherapy can be coupled with chemotherapy or immunotherapy to improve the success rate and increase the patient survival in cancer. In both PDT and PTT, photosensitizes (PSs) or photothermal agents (PTAs) are first exposed to light of appropriate wavelength and electron transition occurs from the ground state (S0) to excited singlet state (S1). After that the electron in S1 state may decay in three different manners; (i) radiative relaxation from S1 state to S0 (fluorescence), (ii) non-radiative relaxation from S1 state to S0 where the energy is released in form of heat (PTT), and (iii) some of the S1 state molecules jump to the excited triplet state T1. The T1 state may further decay in four different manners; (i) radiative relaxation from T1 state to S0 (phosphorescence), (ii) non-radiative relaxation from T1 state to S0 where the energy is released in form of heat (PTT), (iii) generations of free radicals or reactive oxygen species (ROS, O2 .-, OH., H2O2) via transfer of electrons to oxygen or water (Type I, PDT), and (iv) generation of highly cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2) via transfer of energy to natural triplet oxygen (3O2) (Type II, PDT) ( Fig. 9
) [41].

However, the hypoxic tumor microenvironment limits the therapeutic efficacy of PDT in cancer since it depends on the oxygen concentration within tissues. Besides, PDT is more common for the treatment of skin disease and superficial tumors, and visible light stimulated PSs are mostly used for this purpose. Visible light also cannot penetrate deep tumor tissues, and limits the therapeutic efficacy of PDT in cancer. Therefore, near infrared (NIR) active organic PSs such as porphyrins, hypocrellin, and phthalocyanine are developed and used in PDT for cancer treatment owing to their high singlet oxygen generation rate and easy synthesis. However, the poor tumor-targeting ability and low water solubility limits the use of those organic PSs in cancer PDT. On the other hand, gold nanorods and several heavy metal oxide semiconductor nanomaterials show strong NIR absorbance and high photothermal conversion efficiency, but still their clinical use is limited because of their cytotoxicity at higher concentrations. Therefore, search for alternative and novel materials is highly warranted for clinical phototherapy in cancer treatment.

Recently carbon dots (CDs) are gaining huge interest of researchers in the field of phototherapy because of their easy synthesis and surface functionalization, ultra-small size (<10 nm), wide absorption spectrum, tunable photoluminescence, high photostability, increased water solubility and biocompatible nature. Many CDs are reported to absorb light and convert it into ROS or thermal energy and used as PSs and PTAs in PDT and PTT. Particularly, CDs that absorb light above 650 nm wavelength or NIR active CDs show deep tissue penetration, thereby exhibiting excellent photodynamic effects and can be used as PSs. Besides, CDs can also conjugate organic PSs by covalent or non-covalent attachment due to the presence of several functional groups on CD surface and facilitate tumor targeting via enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. Recently several CDs and their composites are designed in a skilful manner with high NIR absorbance, increased photothermal conversion efficiency and excellent biocompatibility by rational selection of precursors and synthesis route. Here we summarized the recent advancement of NIR-responsive CDs for PDT/PTT, the precursor molecules and the target cell types (Table 5–7
).

Ji et al. synthesized red emissive carbon nanodots (RCNDs) from polythiophene phenylpropionic acid via hydrothermal method. The RCNDs were further conjugated with folic acid (FA) via using amine-terminated triethylene glycol (TEG) as a linker. The as-prepared spherically shaped RCNDs-TEG-FA with size ∼ 5 nm exhibited excellent colloidal stability and a wide range stable fluorescence emission spectrum from 500 to 800 nm. RCNDs-TEG-FA increased ROS production upon exposure to 660 nm laser with power 0.1 W.cm −2. The authors further demonstrated that RCNDs-TEG-FA acts as type II photosensitizers and generated 1O2 with a quantum yield of 0.4 via the energy transfer to 3O2, thereby indicating a great potential for PDT. The same RCNDs-TEG-FA were also found to be efficient in the production of intracellular ROS in HeLa cells under the similar exposure condition for 10 min. Finally, the cytocompatibility and PDT efficiency of RCNDs-TEG-FA within a concentration range of 1–––100 µg/mL were checked. RCNDs-TEG-FA displayed only negligible cytotoxicity in HeLa cells after 24 hrs of exposure in the absence of laser irradiation, thereby indicating excellent cytocompatibility. On the other hand, under laser irradiation RCNDs-TEG-FA showed significant cytotoxicity, thereby indicating highly efficient PDT agent to kill tumor cells [42].

Wen et al. prepared NIR responsive spherical CDs with average size ∼ 5.5 nm from pheophytins via microwave irradiation. The CDs exhibited two absorption peaks (411 and 664 nm) and a strong emission peak at 680 nm. Besides, CDs also produced 1O2 with a quantum yield of 0.62 under 671 nm laser irradiation with power 0.1 W.cm −2. The water solubility of the as-prepared CDs was further improved via the introduction of DSPE-mPEG2000. The self-assembled and DSPE-mPEG2000 coated CDs show spherical shape (size ∼ 60 nm) with no significant change in absorption and emission properties. The CD assembly was also found to be able to generate 1O2 under 671 nm laser irradiation with power 0.1 W.cm −2 at both cell free and intracellular conditions (4 T1 cells, exposure time 3 mins). The CD assembly exhibited no cytotoxicity in 4 T1 cells upto the concentration of 250 µg/mL after 24 hrs of exposure in the absence of laser irradiation, whereas ∼ 95 % cell death occurred at the same concentration when exposed to laser irradiation (671 nm, 0.1 W.cm −2 for 10 mins). The CD assembly also showed strong tumor targeting ability due to EPR effect in 4 T1 tumor bearing mice and excellent photodynamic therapeutic efficiency after 14 days of treatment (671 nm laser irradiation with power 0.1 W.cm −2 for 10 mins per day) in reducing the tumor growth without showing any sign of systemic toxicity [43].

Wang et al. fabricated a nanohybrid (GUCNP) by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of up conversion nanoparticles (UCNPs). GQDs were prepared from glutathione and formamide via hydrothermal method followed by coating with polyacrylic acid. GUCNP exhibited enhanced NIR fluorescence and NIR light activated 1O2 generation due to FRET from UCNPs to GQDs. Here, active 1O2 generation was increased because the wavelength of green fluorescence of UCNP matched with the excitation band of GQDs. Upon 980 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W.cm −2 for 10 mins), the nanohybrid system exhibited ∼ 41 % apoptotic cell death in 4 T1 cells due to excessive ROS production. Similarly, the nanohybrid system also showed significant reduction of tumor growth in 4 T1 tumor bearing mice under a 980 nm laser irradiation (power 1.0 W.cm −2 for 10 mins per day), whereas no antitumor activity was found in the absence of laser irradiation [44].

Zhao et al. synthesized red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) with size ∼ 3 nm from o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid by hydrothermal method. This RCDs displayed maximum emission at 622 nm independent of excitation wavelength and produced 1O2 under 532 nm laser irradiation (power 100 mW.cm −2 for 8 min) in a time-dependent manner. The RCDs showed excellent cancer cell killing property towards A549 cells within the concentration range of 5–––40 µg/mL when exposed to 532 nm laser irradiation. Besides, the RCDs significantly inhibited tumor growth in mice under laser irradiation without showing any toxicity to other vital body organs [45].

Jia et al. fabricated CD nanosphere (CDNS) from polythiophene from hydrothermal method and the self-assembled CDNS with size 63 nm were formed further by interaction with sodium dodecylbenzenesulphonate (SDBS) and HOOC-PEG-NH2. The assembled CDNS exhibited broad absorption band from 400 − 800 nm and a strong emission peak at 730 nm under 520 nm excitation. CDND also showed 1O2 generation under 671 nm laser irradiation for 10 mins in cell free condition as well as inside 4 T1 cells (60 mW.cm −2). The assembled CDNS did not display any cytotoxicity in 4 T1 cells upto a concentration of 200 ug/mL without irradiation, whereas ∼ 100 % cell death has occurred at the concentration of 200 ug/mL under 671 nm laser irradiation (60 mW.cm −2). CDNS also exerted in vivo photodynamic therapeutic efficacy in reducing tumor growth in tumor bearing mice when treated for 14 days under 671 nm laser irradiation daily for 10 mins with power 60 mW.cm −2 [46].

Recently in 2022, Yang et al. prepared TCDs with average size 2.5 nm (spherical) from tea polyphenols via solvothermal method. The as-prepared TCDs showed dual-colour fluorescence emission (blue and red) when excited within 360–––460 nm and 500–––620 nm wavelengths respectively.The red emission (maximum peak at 673 nm) was found to be independent of excitation wavelengths. TCDs could also generate.OH and 1O2 under visible light irradiation (400–––500 nm, 15 mW.cm −2), therefore it could act as both type I and type II photosensitizer. It was found that TCDs killed 4 T1 cells in a concentration-dependent manner (50–––300 µg/mL) under visible light irradiation (400–––500 nm, 15 mW.cm −2) for 12 mins, whereas no significant cytotoxicity was observed under dark. Besides, TCDs also significantly inhibited tumor growth in vivo under visible light irradiation (400–––500 nm, 15 mW.cm −2) daily 12 mins for 14 days [47].

In 2023, Zhang et al. fabricated red emissive CDs (RCDs) with tunable type I and type II ROS generation ability from hypericum perforatum extract and polyethylene imine via solvothermal method using the solvent system, water and or ethanol. The synthesized RCDs showed average particle size ∼ 4 nm with absorbance ranging from 600 − 650 nm and a red emission (maximum peak at 680 nm). All the synthesized RCDs exhibited significant ROS generation ability via the production of O2 − • and.OH (type I pathway) as well as 1O2 (type II pathway) under 635 nm laser irradiation with power 50 mW.cm − 2 for 5–––10 min in both cell free system and intracellular conditions. Besides, RCDs were also able to kill 4 T1 cells in a concentration-dependent manner (50–––200 µg/mL) under laser irradiation but did not impart any cytoxicity in the absence of laser light. The authors have further shown that the RCDs could kill 4 T1 cells under hypoxic conditions as well via activation of type I PDT pathway. RCDs also showed significant photodynamic therapeutic efficacy in in vivo condition in inhibiting the tumor growth in mice [48].

In another study, Li et al. synthesized NIR emitting CDs with size ∼ 10 nm from chlorin e6 and polyethylene imine via solvothermal method. The prepared CDs showed three absorption peaks centered at 400, 500 and 662 nm and stable NIR luminescence upto 2 h. ROS or 1O2 generation ability of CDs was also shown in cell free as well as intracellular condition after irradiation with a 685 nm laser (0.1 W.cm − 2) for 4 mins. Besides, the CDs exhibited significant in vitro and in vivo anticancer photodynamic effect after 685 nm laser with power 0.6 W.cm − 2 and 0.8 W.cm − 2 respectively [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: R/NIR CDs used for delivery of photosensitizers
ce:section.ce:para: Xue et al. prepared natural biomass derived carbon dots (NBCDs) from lychee exocarp via pyrolysis method as the photosensitizer carrier. The as-prepared CDs displayed spherical shape with average size of 1.4 nm and the surface was modified by polyethylene glycol diamine. After that NBCD-PEG were further conjugated with photosensitizer molecules, chlorin e6 (Ce6) and navigation molecules, transferrin (Tf) for tumor targeted PDT. This NBCD-PEG-Ce6-Tf displayed maximum emission at 650 nm independent of excitation wavelength and produced 1O2 under 650 nm laser irradiation (power 50 m W.cm −2 for 8 mins). NBCD-PEG-Ce6-Tf showed very low toxicity (∼5 %) towards Bel-7404 cells in the absence of laser within the concentration range of 1–––15 µg/mL, whereas ∼ 90 % cell death occurred when the cells are exposed with 650 nm laser irradiation (power 50 mW.cm −2 for 5 mins) in presence of 15 µg/mL NBCD-PEG-Ce6-Tf. The in vivo antitumor activity was also evaluated, and it was found that NBCD-PEG-Ce6-Tf significantly inhibited the tumor growth in mice under the 650 nm laser irradiation for 10 min daily (power density of 100 mW.cm −2) for 21 days [50].

Sun et al. prepared red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) from citric acid and formamide via microwave-assisted solvothermal method. The RCDs displayed average diameter of ∼ 4 nm and strong fluorescence emission near 640 nm which was independent of excitation wavelength. RCDs showed a concentration-dependent (20–––200 µg/mL) photothermal effect under 671-nm laser irradiation (2.5 W.cm − 2 for 10 min) with high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE = 43.9 %). Approximately 60 °C temperature was increased within 10 mins at a concentration of 200 µg/mL. Besides, RCDs exhibited photothermal anticancer activity in MCF-7 cells both in a concentration and time-dependent manner under 671-nm laser irradiation (2.5 W.cm − 2). On the other hand, RCDs did not impart any considerable cytotoxicity in the absence of laser irradiation upto the concentration of 200 µg/mL [32].

Geng et al. fabricated N and O co-doped carbon dots (NOCDs) with average diameter of ∼ 2 nm from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and branched polyethylenimine via hydrothermal method. The NOCDs showed broad range optical absorption from 600 − 850 nm and a high PCE of 38.3 % under NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W.cm −2) irradiation. The change in temperature was observed as 32.2 °C at a concentration of 200 µg/mL after 5 mins laser irradiation. The NOCDs exerted significant cytotoxicity in Hela cells under the under NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W.cm −2) irradiation for 5 mins with only 13 % cell viability observed after treatment with 200 ug/mL of concentration. Besides, NOCDs also showed in vivo photothermal anticancer activity in tumor bearing mice exposed with NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W.cm −2) irradiation daily for 5 mins for 18 consecutive days [51].

Bao et al. fabricated Cu/CD-crosslinked nanosheets (CuCD NSs) with improved NIR absorption and PCE (41.3 %) for cancer therapy. At first, they synthesized S doped CDs from o-phenylenediamine and L-cysteine via hydrothermal method and then CuSO4 was added to get the desired (CuCD NSs). CuCD NSs showed average size of ∼ 23.4 nm with strong absorbance in NIR region. The CuCD NSs were further modified with thiolated PEG2k to improve their colloidal stability. The PEG-modified CuCD showed ∼ 52 °C temperature elevation at a concentration of 30 µg/mL after 10 mins irradiation with 808 nm laser (2 W.cm −2). Besides, PEG-modified CuCD also exhibited photothermal cytotoxicity in MCF-7 cells as well as in vivo tumor model when irradiated with 808 nm laser for 10 min with power 2 W. cm−2 [52].

In 2022, Najaflu et al. synthesized similar kind of Cu-doped CDs (Cu-dCDs) with size of ∼ 3 nm from Alcea leaves extract and CuSO4 via hydrothermal method. Cu-dCDs showed very high optical absorbance at 800 nm with PCE of ∼ 40 % and were able to increase the temperature by ∼ 50 °C under 800 nm laser irradiation with power 1 W.cm −2 within 8 mins. The cell viability was decreased to 15 % when 4 T1 cells were treated with Cu-dCDs at a concentration of 80 ppm under laser irradiation for 8 mins, whereas more than 85 % cells were alive at the same concentration without irradiation [53].

Ge et al. prepared red emissive CDs with size ∼ 10 nm from polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA) via hydrothermal method. The CDs displayed a broad absorption band from 400 − 800 nm and PCE of 38.5 % under 671 nm laser irradiation. The CDs also increased the temperature of water when dispersed in it and the temperature elevation was found to be ∼ 26.6 °C at a concentration of 200 µg/mL after 10 mins irradiation with 671 nm laser (2 W.cm −2). A concentration dependent phototoxic effect in Hela cells and in vivo phototherapeutic activity in tumor bearing mice were observed under the laser irradiation [54].

Li et al. synthesized CDs with NIR-II emission (900 to 1200 nm under 808 nm laser excitation) from watermelon juice via hydrothermal method. The CDs exhibited wide absorption spectrum from 400 − 800 nm and PCE of 30.6 % under 808 nm laser irradiation. The as-prepared CDs were able to increase the temperature of water by 25 °C at a concentration of 20 mg/mL after 8 mins irradiation with 808 nm laser (1.4 W.cm −2). The CDs displayed significant cytotoxicity in Hela cells under 808 nm laser irradiation, whereas no considerable cytotoxicity was observed upto the concentration of 20 mg/mL under dark. Besides, CDs also effectively reduced the tumor growth in mice within 6 days under the similar laser irradiation condition [28].

Zhao et al. synthesized lysosome targetable CDs with average diameter of ∼ 4.4 nm from nitro coronene via hydrothermal method. The CDs showed broad range absorption spectrum from UV to NIR region and very high PCE of 54.7 % at 808 nm laser irradiation. CDs also showed photothermal toxicity in 4 T1 cells under 808 nm laser irradiation with power 2 W cm−2 [55].

Recently in 2023, Khakbaz et al. synthesized NIR responsive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with size ∼ 2 nm from urea and citric acid via hydrothermal method. CQDs showed very high optical absorbance value at 650 nm with increasing amount of urea. Besides, PCE value of 24.3 % was observed for CQDs under 808 nm laser irradiation (power 1 W.cm −2 for 10 mins). The PTT efficiency of CQDs was found to be 46 %, 44 %, and 48 % in MCF-7, MDA-MB-231, SKBR-3 cells respectively under the similar laser irradiation conditions [56].

Lee et al. synthesized graphene quantum dots via top-down and bottom-up approaches via oxidation of reduced graphene oxide using sodium hypochlorite (RGQDs) and heating hyaluronic acid 300 °C for 5 min (HGQDs). The synthesized RGQDs and HGQDs showed mean diameter of ∼ 10 nm and ∼ 5 nm respectively with substantial NIR absorption and fluorescence in the visible and NIR region. RGQDs and HGQDs were found to increase the temperature of aqueous suspension at concentrations 1.5 mg/mL and 1.7 mg/mL respectively within 10–––15 mins under 808 nm laser irradiation with power of 0.9 W.cm −2. Besides, HGQDs and RGQDs also facilitated heating of HeLa cells up to 43.5 °C and 54.5 °C respectively, and consequently the cell viability was decreased to ∼ 40 % and ∼ 20 % respectively under laser irradiation for 10 mins [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: R/NIR CDs for synergistic cancer PDT/PTT
ce:section.ce:para: Sun et al. synthesized Ce6-modified red emissive carbon dots (Ce6-RCDs) for synergistic cancer photodynamic and photothermal therapy. Red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) were prepared from citric acid and polyethylenimine via solvothermal method first, and then conjugated with 0.56 % mass of Ce6 to form Ce6-RCDs with size ∼ 4 nm. Both RCDs and Ce6-RCDs exhibited broad absorption band from 440 − 750 nm. Ce6-RCDs showed efficient 1O2 generation ability as well as photothermal ability (PCE of 46 %) after 671 nm laser irradiation for 15 min with power 0.50 W.cm −2. Besides, Ce6-RCDs exerted efficient in vitro (in Hela, MCF-7, and 4 T1 cells) and in vivo cancer cell killing activity as compared with single PDT (Ce6) or PTT (RCDs) effect in a synergistic manner (671 nm laser irradiation, 500 mW cm−2). The intratumoral temperature was found to increase from 33.5 to 48.8 °C within 2 mins of exposure [58].

Lan et al. fabricated CDs with absorption range upto 1100 nm from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and Na2SO3 by hydrothermal method. The as-prepared CDs showed average size ∼ 5.3 nm and generated 1O2 via two photon excitation mechanism with a quantum yield of 7.3 %. Similarly, the CDs also showed excellent photothermal conversion capability with PCE of 52.3 % under 800 nm femtosecond pulsed laser irradiation. The CDs did not exhibit any cytotoxicity upto the concentration of 100 µg/mL in Hela cells when treated for 24 hrs in dark and only 20 % cells undergo death at the concentration of 200 µg/mL, whereas the cell viability reached 10 % under 800 nm laser irradiation thereby indicating excellent phototoxicity. Similarly, in vivo tumor ablation property of the CDs was also documented by the authors in 4 T1 murine breast tumor model [59].

Guo et al. prepared Cu and N-doped carbon dots (Cu, N-CDs) with size ∼ 3.3 nm from ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt and cupric chloride via hydrothermal method. Cu, N-CDs exhibited broad optical absorption from 550 − 900 nm with an absorption maximum ∼ 726 nm and excellent photothermal effect under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.0 W.cm −2). The temperature of the aqueous solution of Cu, N-CDs was changed from 25 to 54 °C within 10 min. Cu, N-CDs displayed 1O2 generation ability both in cell free as well as intracellular conditions under 808 nm laser irradiation. A concentration dependent phototoxicity was observed in B16 cells due to synergistic PDT and PTT effect. Cu, N-CDs also showed tumor growth inhibition in tumor bearing mice and were able to increase the intratumoral temperature by 24 °C within 10 mins of laser irradiation [60].

Recently in 2022, Yue et al. prepared CD-based hydrogel by the Schiff base reaction between HA-CHO and CDs for simultaneous PTT and PTT. At first they prepared the CDs by hydrothermal treatment of the aqueous solution of Zinc tetraphenylporphyrin, ethylenediamine and europium(III) chloride. HA-CHO was prepared by the oxidation of hyaluronic acid byNaIO4. Here, the NH2 rich CDs and CD@Hydrogel both acted as photosensitizer as well as photothermal agent (PCE of 37 %) under 660 nm laser irradiation. CD@Hydrogel also showed a significant photo-induced anticancer activity both in vitro and in vivo (temperature change from 35.2 to 48.6 °C within 5 min) [61].

In 2023, Song et al. designed red emitting carbon dots (RCDs) with average size ∼ 4.3 nm via solvothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine and conjugated them with Ce6 to form RCDs-Ce6 for synergistic PDT and PTT effect. Both RCDs and RCDs-Ce6 exhibited strong absorption band extended to 750 nm with PCE of 23.27 % and 16.28 % respectively under 655 nm laser irradiation (500 mW.cm −2). However, RCDs did not produce ROS under laser irradiation, whereas RCDs-Ce6 produced significant amount of ROS compared with free Ce6. RCDs-Ce6 showed PDT and PTT effect in 4 T1 cells under laser irradiation for 5 mins and exerted > 80 % cell death at a concentration of 10 µg/mL, whereas > 80 % cells were alive under daylight exposure. Besides, RCDs-Ce6 exhibited higher safety and efficacy as compared with free Ce6. Besides, RCDs-Ce6 exerted significant tumor ablation property in in vivo model and increased the intratumoral temperature by 15.5 °C within 6 min of laser irradiation [62].

Shinde et al. prepared red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) from Clitoria ternatealeave extract via hydrothermal method and thereafter coated with soya lecithin (lipid) to increase their hydrophilicity and biocompatibility. The lipid coated carbon dots (LRCDs) exhibited average hydrodynamic size of ∼ 210 nm with strong absorption band extended upto 800 nm. LRCDs showed higher PTT and PDT effect as compared with RCDs under 690 nm laser irradiation. LRCDs exhibited temperature elevation above 47 °C under 690 nm laser irradiation for 5 mins and the temperature was maintained upto 10 mins under stable NIR laser [63].

From the discussion, it is evident that NIR active CDs can exhibit both inherent photodynamic as well as photothermal effects and are also useful for the delivery of organic photosensitizers to tumor site via active targeting or EPR effect. However, most of the reported NIR active CDs are prepared from non-renewable or chemical sources. Besides, not much studies are reported regarding active tumor targeting. Therefore, more research works are needed to explore the possible use of green synthesized NIR active CDs with active tumor targeting property for synergistic PDT and PTT without systemic toxicity.

Motivated by the potential applications in biology, clinical practice, pathology, and diagnostics, there is a significant focus on preparing and characterizing red/near-infrared (R/NIR) carbon dots (CDs) with tailored properties. This article compiles reports showcasing emerging uses in sensing, bioimaging, and photodynamic therapy. To sum up, the easy customizability of surface properties in R/NIR CDs makes them a prime choice among optically active zero-dimensional nanomaterials for imaging-based theragnostics and sensor-based research. Moreover, the precise tracking capability of drug-functionalized CDs within cells positions them as excellent programmable therapeutic agents. They exhibit potential for PDT, PTT and ROS generation, promising targeted and non-targeted treatments. Consequently, due to these reasons, researchers worldwide have selected R/NIR CDs for advanced investigations, particularly in challenging environments like tumor cells. However, certain limitations must be acknowledged despite remarkable progress. Achieving predictable structures and desired properties while controlling functional groups, defect positions, and sizes necessitates systematic exploration. The absence of a well-established nucleation and growth mechanism for R/NIR CDs underscores the need for thorough experimentation and computational studies. Attaining accuracy in targeting non-healthy cells during in vivo monitoring demands meticulous scrutiny and corrective strategies. As advancements in R/NIR CDs continue, integrated applications such as drug delivery systems could be realized by recognizing multiple receptor molecules simultaneously.

Overcoming biological and immunological barriers during in vivo studies is also crucial. To enhance clinical compatibility, proper surface functionalization is essential. Preparing CDs emitting in the NIR range (600 – 1800 nm) would facilitate deeper light penetration with minimal scattering and photodamage. Incorporating artificial intelligence (AI) for data analysis, along with additional experiments, will aid in identifying suitable R/NIR CDs boasting water solubility at biological pH, stability, biocompatibility, and multiphoton emission ability. In this comprehensive review, noteworthy findings are summarized, providing insights into chemical, biomedical sensing, imaging, therapeutic, and clinical procedures.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Soumabha Bag: Dr. Soumabha Bag is presently working as Assistant Professor in the Department of Industrial Chemistry, Mizoram University, Aizawl, Mizoram, India. He earned M. Sc. and Ph. D. from the Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai, India. Surface Chemistry at molecular solids and instrument development was his main research area during Ph. D. He moved to Purdue University, IN, USA with his first postdoctoral assignment where he worked on the method development based on portable mass spectrometer. Later, in Karlsruhe Institute and Technology and Ruhr University of Bochum, Germany, he continued to work on the area of molecular solids and alloys and investigated their phases, chemical and electrochemical properties. His current research interest includes synthesis of high entropy oxides, chemical applications of amorphous alloys, method development for the solid waste components separation etc.

Dr. Ashok Pabbathi: Dr. Ashok Pabbathi is currently working as an Assistant Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Central University of Karnataka, India. Before that he served as Assistant Professor at the Department of Industrial Chemistry, Mizoram University, India. He received M.Sc. and Ph.D. in Chemistry from School of Chemistry, University of Hyderabad, Hyderabad, India. He pursued research in single molecule fluorescence spectroscopy during his PhD. Later, he moved for his postdoctoral research at Technion-Israel Institute of Technology, Israel, University of Nebraska Medical Centre, USA and Clemson University, USA. During postdoctoral research he carried out research in single molecule biophysics field. His current research interests include biocatalysis, non-aqueous enzymology and fluorescence spectroscopic investigation of various biomolecular systems.

Dr. Joydeep Das: Dr. Joydeep Das is currently working as an Assistant Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Mizoram University, Aizawl, Mizoram, India. He received M.Sc. from Jadavpurpur University, Kolkata, India and Ph.D. from Bose Institute, Kolkata, India. During his Ph.D., he worked on the extraction of small bioactive natural products, and investigated their beneficial role against drug and toxin-induced organ pathophysiology, type 1 diabetes and its associated organ pathophysiology. During his post-doctoral research in Konkuk University, Seoul, South Korea, he carried out research on nanoparticle synthesis for targeted gene and drug delivery. His current research interests include nanoparticles mediated targeted drug delivery for anticancer and antibacterial purpose; exploration on the mechanism of drug action; and designing nanomaterials for Catalytic and Sensing applications.
